summary:
One-way ANOVA should be used because it assesses variations due to a single factor (different degrees of smoking) while there are no additional interactive elements needed; this method appropriately compares average peak flows amongst specified categories like nonsmokers versus all other smoke exposure intensities mentioned. To ensure clarity regarding patient care implications, focus will remain solely upon evaluating these specific subgroups through univariate analysis rather than multivariable approaches unnecessary under stated circumstances herein described thoroughly above.
The investigation requires employing *a* one-way ANOVA to compare mean peak flow values across diverse smoking statuses accurately reflecting individual contributions towards overall respiratory function metrics observed clinically pertinent parameters involved distinctly isolated via such straightforward yet powerful inferential statistics tool application recommended strongly throughout discussion presented thus far comprehensively addressing core issue at hand efficiently. Agent1 concludes recommending use of "One Way ANOVA" after reviewing supporting documentation indicating its suitability when dealing exclusively with comparisons involving unique but unrelated
